description_ch:
Vehicle license plates of Switzerland, are composed of a two-letter code for the canton and a number with up to 6 digits. The rear plates also display two shields with the flags of Switzerland and the respective canton.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Switzerland
vanity_plates_description_ch:
In Switzerland, license plates are generally issued by the cantonal authorities in ascending order of number, although special plates are possible to a limited extent. If a number is available, it can be obtained for a fee (which varies depending on the number of digits). In most cantons, special numbers are now auctioned off to the highest bidder and fetch even higher prices

Source (German): https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wunschkennzeichen#Nationales
description_de:
Vehicle registration plates (German: Kraftfahrzeug-Kennzeichen or, more colloquially, Nummernschilder) are mandatory alphanumeric plates used to display the registration mark of a vehicle registered in Germany. A significant feature of German vehicle registration plates is the area code, which can be used to tell the district of registration. It has developed into a widespread habit in Germany, even a children's game when travelling, to guess "where that vehicle is from".

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany
vanity_plates_description_de:
For an extra charge of €10.20, vehicle owners can register a personalised identifier, keeping to the above rules. In most cases of personalised plates, owners choose their initials and a number reflecting their date of birth. In this fashion, fictional Mrs Ulrike Mustermann, born 2 May 1965 and living in Essen, might choose E-UM 2565 for her car. By combining area code and random letters, further possibilities arise, such as a man from Oldenburg named Olaf, born on Christmas Eve, could choose OL-AF 2412. A resident of the town of Pirna might choose PIR-AT 77, Pirat meaning "pirate" in German. Kiel and Kleve are two examples of places where the number plate can spell out all of the city name.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany#Personalised_plates
description_at:
The licence plates are made of metal; the imprinted text is in black letters and digits on a white background. Since November 1, 2002 the common design comprises a blue section on the left with the EU circle of stars and the country code ('A') like other vehicle registration plates of the European Union. On the top and bottom, there are red-white-red tribands, the national colours of Austria. Two plates have to be present on each car (front and rear). Dealer plates show white letters on a green background, temporary plates show white letters on a cyan background, and foreign trailers show white letters on a red background. For motorbikes and cars with smaller areas for plates, smaller licence plates are available with two lines of text. Moped plates are in different appearance and shape, they show white letters on a red background.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Austria
vanity_plates_description_at:
In Austria, it is possible to obtain a customized registration plate by payment of €228.30 for registration and €21.00 for the plates themselves. An example of a customized plate is XX ∇ ABC 1. In general, the alphanumeric combination that goes after the coat of arms must consist of at least three characters and begin with a letter and end with a number. The letters and numbers must each be grouped together in a block. This makes such plates easily distinguishable from standard ones.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Austria
description_cz:
Vehicle registration plates of the Czech Republic use a regional code system introduced in 2001. Each plate contains a single letter in the second position of the first group (format: 1A2 3456) that identifies one of the 14 administrative regions (kraje). For example, plates with the letter "A" originate from Prague (Hlavní město Praha), while "B" indicates South Moravia (Jihomoravský kraj).

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_the_Czech_Republic
vanity_plates_description_cz:
In the Czech Republic, personalised registration plates are possible. The owner may choose a combination of letters and numbers subject to availability and regulations set by the regional authorities.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_the_Czech_Republic
description_sk:
Vehicle registration plates of Slovakia used a two-letter district code system from 1997 to 2022. The first two letters identified the district of registration — for example, "BA" for Bratislava I and "KE" for Košice I. In January 2023, Slovakia switched to a national sequential numbering system where the letters no longer indicate the region of registration. The district codes listed here reflect the historical 1997–2022 system.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Slovakia
vanity_plates_description_sk:
Personalised plates have been available in Slovakia since 1998. The format consists of a two-letter prefix (often a former district code, e.g. BA for Bratislava) followed by up to five letters or digits. Unlike standard plates, personalised plates may use the letters Q and W. Plates may not display political, religious, or offensive content. After the 2023 system change, the old district codes are commonly used as the personal prefix even though they no longer appear on standard plates.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Slovakia
description_pl:
Vehicle registration plates of Poland use a county (powiat) code system. Each plate begins with a 2–3 letter code: the first letter identifies the voivodeship (province), and the following letter(s) identify the specific county or city within that voivodeship. For example, "WA" indicates Warsaw in the Masovian Voivodeship, and "KR" indicates Kraków in the Lesser Poland Voivodeship.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Poland
vanity_plates_description_pl:
In Poland, personalised registration plates are available. Owners can apply for a custom combination of letters and numbers, subject to availability and payment of the applicable fee.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Poland
description_ua:
Vehicle registration plates of Ukraine use a two-letter regional code system introduced in 1995. The first two letters identify the oblast (region) of registration — for example, "AA" and "KA" indicate Kyiv City, and "BC" and "HC" indicate Lviv Oblast. Only letters that are visually identical in both Cyrillic and Latin scripts are used (A, B, C, E, H, I, K, M, O, P, T, X). As the number of registered vehicles grew, additional series were introduced in 2018 and subsequent years, so several two-letter codes can map to the same region. Special codes exist for electronic registrations (DI, II) and diplomatic vehicles (CD).

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Ukraine
vanity_plates_description_ua:
Personalised registration plates are available in Ukraine. Owners may apply for a custom combination of letters and digits subject to availability and payment of the applicable fee.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Ukraine
description_n:
Norwegian vehicle registration plates have used a two-letter geographic prefix system since 1 April 1971. The prefix identifies the traffic office (trafikkstasjon) where the vehicle was registered, and therefore indicates the region of registration. In addition to the geographic codes, Norway assigns specific prefixes to vehicles by fuel or propulsion type (electric, gas, hydrogen), as well as separate codes for diplomatic and military vehicles.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Norway
vanity_plates_description_n:
Norway introduced personalised plates (egendefinerte kjennemerker) on 15 June 2017. The one-time fee is NOK 9,000, which grants a 10-year exclusive right to the chosen combination. Plates may be 2–7 characters (letters and digits); the letters G, I, O, and Q are not permitted on any Norwegian plate. Combinations resembling offensive words, registered trademarks, or the standard sequential format are rejected. Eligibility is limited to privately owned passenger cars and caravans — leased vehicles are excluded, and the applicant must be the sole owner and at least 18 years old.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Norway
